content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                322-5901             322-6441              322-6444

December 27, 1977
77-12-086

John Stern
576 - 7th Street
Huntington Beach, CA  92648

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 77-039

Dear Mr. Stern:

     Thank you for your letter of November 1, 1977,
requesting an opinion regarding your duties as a public
official under the Political Reform Act. Because your re-
quest does not raise a substantial question of interpreta-
tion under the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion will
be issued. However, I hope the following comments, provided
pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18329, will be helpful.

     The facts as we understand them are as follows.
You are a Planning Commissioner for the City of Huntington
Beach and are employed by Mariner Savings and Loan Associa-
tion ("Mariner") as a Project Coordinator. At some time,
Mariner may be involved as a lender, joint venturer or sole
developer of projects coming before the Planning Commission.
You have stated that, in your capacity as a Mariner employee,
you have no involvement or information with respect to the
approval process for Mariner's loans.

     Before we address your specific questions, some
background information may be helpful. The Political Reform
Act provides in Government Code Section 87100 1/ that:

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

___________________________
     1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

John Stern
December 27, 1977
Page Two


Section 87103 defines financial interest in perti-
nent part as follows:

    An official has a financial interest in a decision
    within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reason-
    ably foreseeable that the decision will have a
    material financial effect, distinguishable from
    its effect on the public generally, on:

                            ***

    (c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is
made; or

    (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
For purposes of this section, indirect investment
or interest means any investment or interest owned
by the spouse or dependent child of a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a public official, by any
business entity controlled by the public official
or by a trust in which he has a substantial interest.
A business entity is controlled by a public official
if the public official, his agents, spouse and
dependent children hold more than fifty percent of
the ownership interest in the entity. A public
official has a substantial interest in a trust
when the official, his spouse and dependent children
have a present or future interest worth more than
one thousand dollars ($1,000).

    To assist in determining what constitutes a material
financial effect the Commission has promulgated 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18702 which is enclosed.

    Keeping the above-mentioned information in mind,
we now turn to your questions:

    1.  May you participate in a decision of whether
        to approve or disapprove a project if at the
        time Mariner has no connection with the project?

John Stern
December 27, 1977
Page Three


The Commission addressed a similar issue in an
opinion requested by the Marin Municipal Water District,
1 PPPC Opinions 198 (No. 75-089, December 4, 1975), which is
enclosed. If pursuant to the standards set forth in that
opinion it is foreseeable that Mariner will benefit from the
approval or disapproval of the project in question, you must
disqualify yourself from participating in such a decision.

2.     Are you disqualified from participating in a
decision on whether to approve or disapprove
a project for which Mariner is the lender, if
you have no personal knowledge of this fact?

Section 87100 (and the Conflict of Interest Code
for the Planning Commission if it has been adopted) provide
that a public official may not make or participate in a
decision which he knows or has reason to know will foresee­
ably and materially affect his financial interests. Thus,
if you do not know, have reason to know or possess facts
which would lead you to believe that Mariner will be foresee­
ably and materially affected by a particular decision,
you need not disqualify yourself.

3.     Is Mariner prohibited from becoming involved
in investments or projects which will come
before the Planning Commission?

The Political Reform Act does not prohibit Mariner
from becoming involved in any investments or projects because
of your position with the Planning Commission. Rather, the
prohibitions of the Act are directed at officials who make
governmental decisions which foreseeably and materially
affect their financial interests. Accordingly, you may be
prohibited from participating in certain official actions


2/    However, where Mariner is the sole developer
of or joint venturer in a project which comes before the
Planning Commission, we cannot conceive of a set of facts
which would allow you to claim that you did not know or have
reason to know of Mariner's interest.

John Stern
December 27, 1977
Page Four


but Mariner still may seek and obtain Planning Commission
approval for its projects.

     If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion
request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
8321. If you have any questions, please contact Alfredo
Flores of the Commission's Conflict of Interests Division.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:AF:TP:plh
Enclosure

John Stern
526 7th Street
Huntington Beach
(714) 642-4000 Office
(714) 960-500L Home
1 November 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Sirs:

Pursuant to Division VI, Title 2, California Administrative Code - Chapter 3, Section 18320 thereof: I hereby submit a request for opinion regarding the following:

Conflict of interest between by positions as Planning Commissioner for the City of Huntington Beach and Project Coordinator for Mariners Savings and Loan Association, Newport Beach. As a Mariners Savings and Loan Association employee, I have no involvement nor information, and little or no contact with those who do, on matters pertaining to the approval process of loans by Mariners Savings and Loan. My capacity limits my involvement to joint venture or sole developer projects, none of which have been nor are currently in the City. Furthermore, I have no personal knowledge of any project (joint venture or sole developer), nor loan, past, present or future in which Mariners is involved.

Given the above, under what conditions would I need to Abstain from Commission Voting:

1.) When Mariners is Lender (of record)?
2.) When Mariners is Joint Venturing?
3.) When Mariners is Sole Developer?

and for each YES, under 1, 2, or 3 herein above;

1.) When I have no information nor knowledge thereof?
2.) In any event?

By way of elucidating events which make this concern most pressing, comments and directions I received from my immediate superior include:

A. As a follow-up to our discussion and to reinforce the employee-employer relationship you have with Mariners and the interrelationship of that to your appointment, you are hereby advised as follows:

a. All your Huntington Beach Planning Commission business and affairs related thereto shall be conducted outside the normal, usual, and expected Mariner's work day and week;

b. Mariners office facilities, staff, services, and supplies shall not be used at any time in your conducting Huntington Beach Planning Commission business and affairs related thereto;

c. In all matters considered, heard, to be decided or recommended by the Huntington Beach Planning Commission, you are to ascer-

Fair Political Practices Commission
1 November 1977
Page 2

tain whether Mariners has a direct, indirect, or potential in-
volvement in such matters;

d. You shall abstain in every way from participating in all mat-
ters considered, heard, to be decided or recommended by the
Huntington Beach Planning Commission which directly, indirectly,
or potentially involve Mariners or Mariner's Borrowers; your
abstention shall include discussions and/or venturing opinions
publicly or privately about all such matters so involving
Mariners; and

e. You shall take every step necessary to preclude any and all
"conflict-of-interest" occurences.

B. You are aware that your appointment has precluded this Department
from becoming involved in any investments and/or projects in Hunt-
ington Beach due to the prima-facia potential of interest conflicts
which would be inherent due to your Mariners employee duties and
relationship to the City staff and Council resulting from your
Planning Commission appointment. This has jeopardized the function-
ing of this Department in a sphere of operations which may not be
beneficial to Mariners.

C. If, as a result of your conduct acting or allegedly acting in
your Huntington Beach Planning Commission capacity, Mariners is a
named party in any litigation or threatened litigation whether
such proceeds to judgement or not, all costs sustained by Mariners
in responding to, answering or defending itself in such litigation
shall be paid by you. In such threatened or actual litigation
Mariners shall retain its right to respond thereto in the manner
it deems most responsible in its sole judgement.

Please consider the above in your opinion, which hopefully can be struc-
tured such that:

1. If at the time of decision, Mariners is not involved in a matter
before the Commission, then the Action taken by the Commission will
stand, regardless of the position (sole developer, joint venture,
or lender) or benefits Mariners assume or accrues relative that
same matter at some later date (an EX POST FACTO release);

2. If, at the time of decision, Mariners is a lender in a matter before
the Commission, then unless I have personal or indirect knowledge of the
Loan or matter, that I can act in my capacity as Commissioner,
with no liability to myself, Mariners, or the City;

3. That B herein above is not true; that my capacity as a Commissioner
in no way precludes "this department" of which I am an employee,
and whose sole function is the development of property, from ini-
tiating, carrying on or assuming responsibilities for development
in the City; and

Fair Political Practices Commission
1 November 1977
Page 3

4. That taken to an absurd extreme, the Conflict of Interest Law could
apply to actions taken when any Principal in any Project before the
Commission (as is a) an account holder at Mariners, b) a Stockholder of
Mariners - listed on the American Stock Exchange, c) has ANY sort
of loan with Mariners, or, d) is involved in another Project with
Mariners. In conversations with Mr. Tom Deemer, Executive Vice-
President of Mariners, I was informed that Mariners' policy pre-
cludes giving information on any of the above (a - d) items to the
public or employees. Consequently, I have no way to determine (a - d)
above, nor is it a matter of public record, unless I ask each Prin-
cipal involved. Mariners does not want me to query anyone accord-
ingly.

Lastly, Section 3625 of the Government Code states in part, "(a) No pub-
lic official shall participate in, or in any way attempt to influence
governmental action or decisions relating to any matter within the re-
sponsibilities of his agency in which he knows or has reason to believe
he has an economic interest. (b) An official has an economic interest
in the matter if the action or decision will have a material economic
effect on: . . . (4) any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any posi-
tion of management. . . ." (emphasis added). I would like to know how a
"material economic effect" should be interpreted with regards to Mariners.
I include herewith a copy of Mariners latest financial report (June 30, '77),
as well as my own Statement of Economic Interests for your consideration in
this question.

Your earliest possible response will be most appreciated by all con-
cerned.

Very truly yours,

John Stern
John Stern

Encl: Mariners Financial Corp., Interim Report, June 30, 1977.
Statement of Economic Interests (for John Stern)
dated 26 July 1977.

sections.question: 1. May you participate in a decision of whether to approve or disapprove a project if at the time Mariner has no connection with the project? 2. Are you disqualified from participating in a decision on whether to approve or disapprove a project for which Mariner is the lender, if you have no personal knowledge of this fact? 3. Is Mariner prohibited from becoming involved in investments or projects which will come before the Planning Commission?
sections.conclusion: 1. If it is foreseeable that Mariner will benefit from approval or disapproval of a project, you must disqualify yourself. 2. If you do not know, have reason to know, or possess facts suggesting Mariner will be foreseeably and materially affected, you need not disqualify yourself. 3. Mariner is not prohibited from becoming involved in projects before the Planning Commission, but you may be prohibited from participating in certain official actions affecting those projects.

sections.question_synthetic: A Planning Commissioner employed by a savings and loan association seeks guidance on whether he must disqualify himself from decisions involving projects where his employer may be involved as a lender, joint venturer, or developer.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the official must disqualify himself when it is reasonably foreseeable that his employer will be materially and foreseeably affected by a planning decision, applying an objective standard of what he knows or has reason to know, not subjective ignorance.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document is a declination letter (no formal opinion issued due to lack of substantial question), but includes substantive guidance on three specific questions. Some pages appear partially illegible or contain handwritten annotations that were difficult to parse completely. Core advice sections are clearly extracted.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May you participate in a decision of whether to approve or disapprove a project if at the time Mariner has no connection with the project? 2. Are you disqualified from participating in a decision on whether to approve or disapprove a project for which Mariner is the lender, if you have no personal knowledge of this fact? 3. Is Mariner prohibited from becoming involved in investments or projects which will come before the Planning Commission?

CONCLUSION: 1. If it is foreseeable that Mariner will benefit from approval or disapproval of a project, you must disqualify yourself. 2. If you do not know, have reason to know, or possess facts suggesting Mariner will be foreseeably and materially affected, you need not disqualify yourself. 3. Mariner is not prohibited from becoming involved in projects before the Planning Commission, but you may be prohibited from participating in certain official actions affecting those projects.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provided informal comments (declining to issue a formal opinion) to a Huntington Beach Planning Commissioner regarding conflict of interest obligations under the Political Reform Act when his employer, a savings and loan association, may seek approvals for projects before the Commission.
